entries.headline: US and Iran at war again as peace deal fails
entries.summary: The peace deal between the US and Iran has broken down. The US attacked over 100 targets in Iran, and Iran fought back at US bases and tried to block a crucial shipping route. Now they're in a fierce battle for control of the waterway.
entries.why: This fight is blocking the main supply of energy for the world, which could quickly make fuel prices go up very high.
entries.fp: us and iran at war again as peace deal fails

entries.headline: Russia bans diesel exports after drone attacks on refineries
entries.summary: Russia has stopped exporting diesel fuel because simple drones from Ukraine have damaged many of its oil refineries. People in Russia are now waiting in long lines for fuel, and the government is even importing fuel from India. Diesel prices in Europe have gone up to record highs because one of the world's biggest sellers is no longer selling.
entries.why: It shows how small, cheap drones can really hurt a powerful country's economy and make transportation more expensive for everyone.
entries.fp: russia bans diesel exports after drone attacks on refineries

entries.headline: Venezuela's twin earthquakes leave thousands dead and cities ruined
entries.summary: Over 4,300 people have died after two huge earthquakes hit Venezuela just 39 seconds apart. The rare 'doublet' quake destroyed entire neighborhoods, and thousands of people are still missing. The UN is trying to raise money to help, but Venezuela is already struggling financially.
entries.why: It's the worst natural disaster in the region in over a century, causing a huge health crisis and displacing thousands of people.
entries.fp: venezuela s twin earthquakes leave thousands dead and cities ruined

entries.headline: Qatar delays global gas supply increase after shipping attacks
entries.summary: Qatar has put on hold its plans to send more natural gas to the world after one of its ships was attacked in the Middle East. The country's main energy facility is now running at very low levels to keep workers safe. Ships are turning back rather than risk going through the dangerous waters.
entries.why: This delay will make it much harder and more expensive for Europe and Asia to heat their homes this winter.
entries.fp: qatar delays global gas supply increase after shipping attacks

entries.headline: Severe El Niño weather threatens global food price surge
entries.summary: Weather experts warn that a severe El Niño cycle could cause a global food crisis that lasts for years. Extreme heat is already causing huge wildfires in Canada and Spain, and droughts are damaging crops. If this continues, the cost of basic groceries could go up worldwide.
entries.why: It means families everywhere will feel the pinch at the supermarket, while poorer countries could face severe food shortages.
entries.fp: severe el ni o weather threatens global food price surge

entries.headline: U.S. and Iran Exchange Heavy Airstrikes and Retaliatory Missile Attacks Across the Persian Gulf
entries.summary: The U.S. military launched a fresh wave of precision airstrikes on dozens of Iranian military targets, aiming to keep the Strait of Hormuz open. In direct retaliation, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) fired missiles and drones targeting U.S. military bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan. The strikes prompted air raid sirens in Bahrain, led Jordan to shoot down four incoming missiles, and forced Kuwait's military to engage hostile targets in its airspace. This major military escalation marks a near-total collapse of the tentative June ceasefire.
entries.why: This is a major military escalation and direct combat between U.S. and Iranian forces that has expanded into a wider regional conflict. It directly threatens global energy security through the declared closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a vital maritime chokepoint for the world's oil and gas supply.
entries.fp: u s and iran exchange heavy airstrikes and retaliatory missile

entries.headline: US and Iran have biggest fight in months near a key shipping route
entries.summary: The US military attacked Iran after a cargo ship was damaged. Iran quickly fought back, targeting US bases with missiles. Iran says a crucial shipping route is closed, but the US disagrees, causing oil prices to rise.
entries.why: This direct fight over a vital oil route could easily disrupt global energy supplies and lead to a bigger war.
entries.fp: us and iran have biggest fight in months near a

entries.headline: Typhoon Bavi hits eastern China, forcing millions to evacuate
entries.summary: Bavi hit China's coast as a strong storm, bringing strong winds and floods. Officials moved over two million people to safety before it struck. The storm has caused power outages, uprooted trees, and disrupted transportation.
entries.why: It's the strongest storm to hit China this year, showing the huge impact of extreme weather on global manufacturing and people's lives.
entries.fp: typhoon bavi hits eastern china forcing millions to evacuate

entries.headline: European heatwave causes over 10,000 deaths
entries.summary: A severe heatwave has caused over 10,000 deaths in Europe. Germany has reported 5,000 victims, and dry conditions have sparked wildfires in Spain. The heat is damaging roads, melting tracks, and overwhelming emergency services.
entries.why: This tragedy shows that even wealthy countries are struggling to cope with the deadly effects of climate change.
entries.fp: european heatwave causes over 10 000 deaths

entries.headline: Philippine Vice President Sara Duterte's impeachment trial continues
entries.summary: The Senate trial of Vice President Sara Duterte is happening in Manila. She's accused of corruption and making threats. If found guilty, she'll lose her job and can't run again, which could change the country's politics.
entries.why: This trial is a big deal that could affect a major democratic country in Southeast Asia.
entries.fp: philippine vice president sara duterte s impeachment trial continues

entries.headline: US Senator Lindsey Graham dies suddenly at 71
entries.summary: Senator Lindsey Graham passed away from a sudden heart condition after returning from a trip to Ukraine. He was a well-known voice on foreign policy and a key supporter of US aid to Ukraine and Israel.
entries.why: His death removes a major figure who supported US military aid to Ukraine and Israel during critical global conflicts.
entries.fp: us senator lindsey graham dies suddenly at 71

entries.headline: U.S. and Iran trade heavy strikes as Strait of Hormuz conflict explodes
entries.summary: The uneasy peace between the U.S. and Iran has completely broken down. Over the last 24 hours, the two sides have traded massive military blows [1.2.2]. It started when Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz—the world’s most important choke point for oil—closed. The U.S. responded with heavy airstrikes to open it back up. Iran then fired missiles and drones at U.S. bases in Kuwait, Jordan, and Bahrain. Now, oil prices are spiking, and there are major fears of a wider war.
entries.why: This is a direct, heavy military clash between two major powers over a global trade artery. It is not a slow-moving political spat; it is active combat that is already pulling in surrounding countries and threatening to mess up the global economy.
entries.fp: u s and iran trade heavy strikes as strait of

entries.headline: US and Iran Exchange Massive Strikes Over Strait of Hormuz as Region Braces for War
entries.summary: Following the collapse of a recent ceasefire, the US military launched intensive airstrikes against over 140 Iranian targets, debuting combat sea drones to strike facilities in Bandar Abbas. In immediate retaliation, Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) launched missile and drone attacks targeting US military bases in Kuwait, Jordan, Bahrain, and Qatar. In response to the clashes, President Donald Trump declared a US naval blockade on Iranian shipping and announced a unilateral 20% toll on cargo crossing the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran has declared closed. The escalations have sent global oil prices surging by 5%.
entries.why: This marks a severe and rapid military escalation between the United States and Iran, transitioning from proxy conflicts to direct, heavy exchange of fire. The active battle over the Strait of Hormuz—the world's most critical maritime energy bottleneck—directly threatens global energy markets and risks pulling the wider Middle East into an open, regional war.
entries.fp: us and iran exchange massive strikes over strait of hormuz

entries.headline: U.S. Reinstates Iran Naval Blockade and Imposes Strait of Hormuz Cargo Toll Amid Massive Military Escalation
entries.summary: Following the collapse of a shaky ceasefire, President Donald Trump announced that the U.S. is reimposing a naval blockade on Iranian ports and will charge a 20% toll on all other cargo transiting the Strait of Hormuz to cover American security costs. This follows a weekend of heavy fighting, featuring U.S. strikes against dozens of Iranian targets—including the first combat use of American sea drones—and retaliatory Iranian strikes targeting U.S. bases and allies in Jordan, Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, and Oman.
entries.why: The Strait of Hormuz is a vital global trade choke point handling a fifth of the world's oil and gas. Reinstating a blockade, imposing an unprecedented unilateral maritime toll, and entering a direct multi-front hot war between the U.S. and Iran marks a major military escalation with severe consequences for global energy markets and international security.
entries.fp: u s reinstates iran naval blockade and imposes strait of

entries.headline: US and Iran in tense standoff over Strait of Hormuz
entries.summary: The US and Iran are in a heated argument over a vital waterway called the Strait of Hormuz. This route is crucial because a fifth of the world's oil passes through it. The US has threatened to block Iranian ships and charge other ships a fee for safety. Iran responded by attacking US military bases in nearby countries. This escalation has halted peace talks, raising fears of a full-scale war.
entries.why: If this waterway closes, it's like blocking a major blood vessel for global energy. This would cause fuel and shipping prices to skyrocket worldwide.
entries.fp: us and iran in tense standoff over strait of hormuz

entries.headline: Venezuela's earthquake crisis turns into survival struggle
entries.summary: Three weeks after massive earthquakes hit northern Venezuela, over 4,300 people have died and tens of thousands are still missing. The disaster caused $37 billion in damage, leaving millions without clean water, power, or food. The country's economy was already struggling, and the government is overwhelmed and unable to lead recovery efforts.
entries.why: This is a huge humanitarian disaster that will likely force millions more people to flee Venezuela, changing the regional refugee crisis.
entries.fp: venezuela s earthquake crisis turns into survival struggle

entries.headline: European summer heatwave kills over 10,000 people
entries.summary: New data shows that a severe summer heatwave in Europe has killed over 10,000 people. Extreme heat is drying up water reservoirs and straining electricity grids. Governments are scrambling to adapt to what is becoming a longer and more deadly summer season.
entries.why: It shows that the climate crisis is no longer a distant threat, but an active, deadly risk to human health and infrastructure in some of the world's wealthiest nations.
entries.fp: european summer heatwave kills over 10 000 people

entries.headline: Severe El Niño and global conflicts may cause food price surge
entries.summary: Scientists and market experts warn that a severe El Niño weather pattern and regional wars could threaten global food security. Extreme weather is ruining crops, while military clashes and shipping blockades are trapping grain supplies. This double threat could keep grocery prices high until 2028.
entries.why: When food gets too expensive, it doesn't just hurt household budgets; it can cause severe hunger and political unrest in the world's poorest countries.
entries.fp: severe el ni o and global conflicts may cause food

entries.headline: The European Union pledges $1 billion to rebuild Gaza, but peace remains elusive
entries.summary: The EU has pledged nearly $1 billion in aid to start rebuilding Gaza, which has been devastated by years of conflict. However, the ceasefire is stalled, and international groups estimate the actual cost to rebuild is at least $70 billion. Clearing the massive debris alone is expected to take over seven years.
entries.why: The pledge highlights the huge gap between the financial aid offered and the reality of a stalled peace process that prevents rebuilding from starting.
entries.fp: the european union pledges 1 billion to rebuild gaza but

entries.headline: US-Iran Ceasefire Collapses Into Direct War With Airstrikes, Strait of Hormuz Blockade, and Attacks on US Bases
entries.summary: The month-old ceasefire between the US and Iran has shattered as both nations resumed direct, high-intensity conflict. Following Iranian attacks on commercial vessels, the US military launched a massive wave of precision airstrikes targeting air defense systems, coastal radars, and military sites inside Iran. In response, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) launched retaliatory ballistic missiles and drones targeting major US military facilities in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan, forcing regional allies to engage active air defenses. Concurrently, US President Donald Trump announced a reinstated naval blockade on Iranian ports and declared a 20% toll on all other cargo ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz. This rapid escalation has paralyzed over 90% of shipping in the strategic waterway and sent global oil prices surging by nearly 10%.
entries.why: This represents a major, direct military confrontation between the United States and Iran that threatens to spiral into an all-out regional war. The conflict has instantly disrupted the world's most critical energy transit chokepoint, triggered an immediate global oil price shock, and forced multiple neighboring sovereign nations to conduct active combat interceptions of incoming missiles.
entries.fp: us iran ceasefire collapses into direct war with airstrikes strait

entries.headline: U.S. and Iran Exchange Heavy Strikes as Trump Orders Naval Blockade on Iranian Ports
entries.summary: A short-lived peace deal between the US and Iran has totally collapsed. The US military is launching a third straight night of heavy airstrikes on Iran. President Trump just announced a full naval blockade on Iranian ports starting Tuesday, and he wants to charge a 20% toll on ships using the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has fired back with missiles and drones hitting US bases and allies in Bahrain, Jordan, and Kuwait.
entries.why: This is a massive military escalation. The Strait of Hormuz is like the world's main oil artery—about a fifth of the world's oil goes through it. A direct war and a US blockade here could choke global trade and send energy prices through the roof.
entries.fp: u s and iran exchange heavy strikes as trump orders

entries.headline: US Strikes Iran for Third Night and Reinstates Blockade as Tanker Attacks in Strait of Hormuz Kill Sailor
entries.summary: The US military has launched a third consecutive night of airstrikes against Iran, while President Trump has ordered a naval blockade on all Iranian ports. In retaliation, Iran used cruise missiles to strike two Emirati oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, killing an Indian sailor and injuring eight others. This dramatic escalation has caused global oil prices to jump sharply.
entries.why: This is a major military escalation that marks the resumption of the US-Iran war. By cutting off the Strait of Hormuz and launching direct strikes, both sides are threatening the world's most vital energy chokepoint, which could trigger a global energy crisis and a much larger regional war.

entries.headline: U.S. and Iran Resume Heavy Fighting as Trump Reinstates Strait of Hormuz Blockade
entries.summary: A short-lived peace deal has completely collapsed. President Trump announced that the U.S. Navy is reinstating a naval blockade on Iranian ports and suggested charging cargo ships a 20% fee to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. The U.S. military has launched its third straight night of major airstrikes on Iranian targets. In response, Iran's Revolutionary Guard hit back, firing missiles at U.S. bases in Bahrain and Jordan and striking two UAE tankers, which killed one crew member.
entries.why: This is a massive military escalation in the world's most crucial oil chokepoint. With the U.S. enforcing a blockade and demanding transit fees, and Iran retaliating directly against U.S. regional bases and commercial tankers, this flare-up threatens to spark a wider regional war and trigger a global energy crisis.

entries.headline: Scientists warn of a super-powerful El Niño event
entries.summary: Meteorologists say there's an 81% chance of a huge climate event that could break heat records. We're already seeing really hot weather and wildfires. This weather pattern acts like a giant heater for the Earth.
entries.why: It could cause severe droughts, floods, and crop failures, leading to food shortages, like when a big storm ruins a farmer's crops.

entries.headline: France helps Ukraine build its own advanced missiles
entries.summary: French President Macron said Ukraine can build advanced French missiles and bombs on its own soil. Ukraine will also buy fighter jets and get pilot training. Europe is now helping Ukraine build its own permanent arms factories.
entries.why: This changes the long-term situation of the war, making Ukraine less dependent on foreign help for its defense, like when you teach someone to fish instead of giving them fish.
entries.fp: france helps ukraine build its own advanced missiles

entries.headline: The US has to refund $81 billion in unfair tariffs
entries.summary: The US Supreme Court said Trump's import taxes were unlawful, so the government has to pay back a lot of money to businesses. This unexpected refund has created a huge hole in the federal budget.
entries.why: It shows how hard it is to use executive orders to run a trade war, and now the US economy has a growing deficit, like when you overspend on a credit card.
entries.fp: the us has to refund 81 billion in unfair tariffs

entries.headline: The US tries to dismantle the International Criminal Court
entries.summary: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio is trying to tear down the world's highest war crimes court. He says the court threatens American sovereignty and could target US border agents and leaders.
entries.why: If the world's most powerful country tries to destroy the global court, it could hurt international justice and human rights accountability for a long time, like when you undermine a referee's authority in a game.

entries.headline: US Strikes Iran as Trump Reinstates Hormuz Blockade and Imposes 20% Shipping Toll
entries.summary: The US has launched a third consecutive night of airstrikes against Iran, with President Donald Trump announcing a reinstated naval blockade on Iranian ports starting Tuesday, July 14, 2026. In a major policy departure, Trump declared the US will charge a 20% fee on all cargo passing through the Strait of Hormuz to cover security costs. Iran has retaliated by striking targets in Bahrain and Jordan, and firing cruise missiles at two UAE-linked oil tankers, killing one crew member and prompting oil prices to spike.
entries.why: This is a massive military escalation that shatters a fragile ceasefire in the 2026 US-Iran war, threatening to drag regional neighbors like the UAE into direct conflict. Additionally, the US imposing a 20% transit fee on ships in the Strait of Hormuz fundamentally upends centuries of global freedom-of-navigation principles and risks severe disruptions to international energy markets.
entries.fp: us strikes iran as trump reinstates hormuz blockade and imposes

entries.headline: US reinstates naval blockade on Iran as Strait of Hormuz conflict reignites
entries.summary: The shaky ceasefire between the US and Iran has collapsed. US forces just finished a third consecutive night of intense airstrikes targeting Iranian defenses, missile sites, and the port city of Bushehr. In retaliation, Iran fired ballistic missiles at a US air base in Jordan and launched cruise missiles at two UAE oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, killing one sailor. To push back, President Trump announced the US is reinstating a strict naval blockade on Iranian ports starting Tuesday at 4 p.m. ET. In a major twist, he also declared the US the 'Guardian' of the strait and said they will charge a 20% fee on all other cargo ships passing through to cover US security costs.
entries.why: The Strait of Hormuz is the world's most important oil transit route, handling a fifth of global energy supply. A renewed naval blockade coupled with active airstrikes, missile retaliation, and a massive 20% shipping fee could easily spiral into a full-scale war, disrupt global trade networks, and send global oil prices skyrocketing.
entries.fp: us reinstates naval blockade on iran as strait of hormuz

entries.headline: U.S. and Iran trade heavy strikes as Trump orders naval blockade and ships are hit in the Gulf
entries.summary: Things just took a very scary turn in the Middle East. The U.S. military hammered targets inside Iran for the third night in a row. This came right after President Donald Trump announced a full naval blockade on Iranian ports and said the U.S. would start charging a 20% fee for ships using the Strait of Hormuz. In response, Iran's military launched cruise missiles that struck two UAE oil tankers, killing a crew member and starting onboard fires. Iran also fired missiles at a U.S. base in Jordan and sent drones toward Bahrain.
entries.why: This is a massive military escalation. We are seeing active fighting between the U.S. and Iran, a full-on naval blockade, and attacks on civilian oil tankers in the world's most important energy choke point. It has sent oil prices spiking and threatens to drag the whole region into a much larger war.

Iran fought back by attacking ships and U.S. military bases. Now, the U.S. is blocking Iranian ports with ships, which is making oil prices go up.

entries.headline: Heat Waves and Wildfires Are Getting Worse
entries.summary: Very hot weather is causing records to be broken, starting huge wildfires, and killing over 10,000 people in Europe and North America. Wildfires are getting close to Paris, burning in Spain, and are out of control in the U.S. and Canada. The heat is also hurting crops, which means Europe won't have as much grain as usual.
entries.why: The heat and fires are directly threatening people's lives, straining power grids, and putting the world's food supply at risk.
entries.fp: heat waves and wildfires are getting worse

entries.headline: Ukraine Teams Up with Europe for Missile Defense
entries.summary: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced a new partnership with nine European countries to build a shared missile defense system. This comes as Ukraine is struggling with a shortage of expensive Western air defense missiles because of constant Russian attacks.
entries.why: This move helps Ukraine have a permanent defense system with its European neighbors, which could help with security after the war.
entries.fp: ukraine teams up with europe for missile defense

entries.headline: New U.S. Law Helps Regular People Buy Homes
entries.summary: A big new housing law has started in the U.S. to help regular families buy homes. The law stops big companies and investors who own a lot of houses from buying more single-family homes. The law went into effect because President Trump didn't sign or veto it.
entries.why: This law tries to stop big companies from outbidding regular people who want to buy starter homes.
entries.fp: new u s law helps regular people buy homes

entries.headline: China's Exports Are Booming Because of AI
entries.summary: China's exports went up by 27% in June because people around the world want artificial intelligence technology. Demand for computer chips, smart devices, and electronic parts grew by almost 57% in the first half of the year. This boom is helping China's economy, which has been struggling because of a housing market problem.
entries.why: It shows how much the AI boom is changing global trade, making technology the main driver of economic growth.
entries.fp: china s exports are booming because of ai

entries.headline: US-Iran Peace Deal Collapses Into Heavy Airstrikes, Blockades, and Missile Attacks

US forces just finished their third straight night of intense airstrikes against Iranian military targets. At the same time, President Trump announced that the US is bringing back a full naval blockade on Iran and plans to charge all cargo ships a 20% 'tax' to sail through the Strait of Hormuz. Iran didn't wait to retaliate—they fired ballistic missiles at a US air base in Jordan, launched attacks near Bahrain, and struck several international oil tankers in the Gulf, killing one crew member. entries.headline: Record-breaking heatwaves and massive wildfires are testing Europe
entries.summary: Europe is experiencing its third extreme heatwave in just six weeks, leading to over 10,000 heat-related deaths. Huge wildfires are burning out of control, including a massive fire near Paris that has closed motorways and forced people to evacuate. Temperatures have soared above 40°C (104°F) in several countries, putting a strain on electricity grids as people try to stay cool.
entries.why: This shows that climate change is not just a future threat, but a crisis that is already affecting people's lives and causing thousands of deaths in some of the world's richest countries.

entries.headline: WHO warns of a rapidly spreading Ebola outbreak in Central Africa
entries.summary: The World Health Organization has raised the alarm about a deadly Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo that is spreading faster than any previous outbreak. Over 700 people have died so far, and because the virus is spreading through unknown paths, it's hard to track. To make matters worse, health workers don't have any approved vaccines or treatments for this particular strain of the virus.
entries.why: If this outbreak isn't contained in Congo, it could easily spread across borders and trigger a major global health emergency.

entries.headline: Europe and Ukraine team up to build a affordable missile shield
entries.summary: Ukraine and nine other European countries have launched a new coalition to build a shared air-defense shield across Europe. Instead of waiting for expensive U.S.-made Patriot missile systems, the group is funding a cheaper, homegrown Ukrainian alternative called Freya. This move comes as Russia intensifies its devastating ballistic missile strikes on Kyiv.
entries.why: This is a significant shift in European defense, showing that European allies are preparing to rely less on America and more on their own cheaper, faster military technology.

entries.headline: Federal judge scraps Trump's $1.8 billion IRS deal
entries.summary: A federal judge in Florida has canceled a controversial settlement that President Trump made with the IRS. The deal would have created a $1.8 billion fund to pay off Trump's allies and given his family lifetime immunity from federal tax audits. The judge criticized the arrangement as 'collusion,' saying that Trump basically sued himself to get tax immunity and taxpayer cash.
entries.why: This is a huge win for the rule of law, proving that even a sitting president cannot use the court system to dodge taxes and fund political allies.
